title: What Is One to Tell? What Is One to Leave Out?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below preserves the current OCR transcription as a reading copy; the scanned source remains authoritative. Mechanical OCR joins such as the broken “single-column story” phrase and split sentences across page boundaries are resolved in English only where their reading is unambiguous. No uncertain Tamil wording has been silently invented, and the canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: At its meeting on 1 June 2015, the Karnataka Cabinet decided that the Karnataka Government should appeal to the Supreme Court in the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa. Every newspaper published in Tamil Nadu carried the news prominently on its front page. In *Dinamani* alone, it appeared in one corner of page nine, in the tiny format known as a “single-column story.” From that, one may understand the importance the paper assigned to it. Various opposition parties welcomed the Karnataka Government's decision. In particular, Tamil Nadu Congress Committee president, younger brother E. V. K. S. Elangovan; Tamil Nadu BJP president Tamilisai Soundararajan; Dravidar Kazhagam president K. Veeramani; MDMK general secretary, younger brother Vaiko; PMK founder Dr Ramadoss; CPI(M) State Secretary Comrade G. Ramakrishnan; CPI State Secretary Comrade Mutharasan; and Tamil Maanila Congress president, younger brother G. K. Vasan all issued statements welcoming the decision.

Speaking to reporters, Karnataka Law Minister T. B. Jayachandra said: “On the recommendations of Special Public Prosecutor B. V. Acharya, Advocate General Ravivarma Kumar and Law Secretary Sangappa, the Cabinet has decided to appeal in Jayalalithaa's case. Chief Minister Siddaramaiah has directed that the appeal be filed promptly in the Supreme Court. There is no intention of political vengeance in this decision. The appeal has been decided entirely on legal grounds. B. V. Acharya will appear and argue for the Karnataka Government in the Supreme Court appeal, assisted by Sandesh Chouta.”

In an interview about the Karnataka Government's decision, B. V. Acharya, who will appear in the Supreme Court, said: “Of the 919 pages in the judgment, 800 merely recount the old events of the case. The remaining 119 pages contain more than a hundred defects. Viewed from that angle too, there is no possibility of all four accused being acquitted. I wrote a recommendation to the Principal Secretary of Karnataka's Law Department and Advocate General Ravivarma Kumar, pointing out the errors in the judgment and urging an appeal to the Supreme Court. The Government considered my reasonable request. I shall demonstrate my ability before the Supreme Court and seek a stay of the Karnataka High Court judgment. I will have that judgment set aside and the Bengaluru Special Court judgment confirmed.”

We were not the first to make an allegation about Jayalalithaa's accumulation of assets worth Rs. 66 crore. According to details presented in the Rajya Sabha, before she first became Chief Minister, her assets on 1 July 1991 were worth Rs. 2,01,83,957. Jayalalithaa served as Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu from 1991 until May 1996. After completing those five years in office, the value of her assets on 30 April 1996 stood at Rs. 66,65,20,395. The case itself was instituted on the basis of these figures presented in Parliament.

During the trial before the Special Court, the accused asked Judge Cunha to deduct certain items from this valuation. Accepting some of those submissions, he based his judgment not on Rs. 66 crore but on assets worth Rs. 53,60,49,954. Jayalalithaa's side appealed against that judgment. Justice Kumaraswamy, who heard the appeal, reduced Judge Cunha's Rs. 53 crore valuation still further, holding that the assets were worth only Rs. 37,59,02,466. He then stated that Jayalalithaa and the others had received income of Rs. 34,76,65,654 to purchase those assets. Deducting that income from the asset value, he calculated that only Rs. 2,82,36,812 had been accumulated beyond income. Since this disproportionate amount was only eight per cent of Jayalalithaa's total income, below ten per cent, he concluded that no punishment was required. If Justice Kumaraswamy's figures had been true, anyone would have accepted the judgment and no controversy of this magnitude would have arisen!

In calculating Jayalalithaa's income of Rs. 34,76,65,654, Justice Kumaraswamy said that Rs. 24,17,31,274 had come from bank loans alone. He also listed the names in which those loans had been taken and their dates. But add the figures he listed, and the total is only Rs. 10,67,36,274.

When only Rs. 10.67 crore came as income through bank loans, can a judgment delivered by a High Court judge acquitting Jayalalithaa after wrongly inflating it to Rs. 24.17 crore be called proper or just? That is what all of India is asking. Have those who benefited from the judgment offered any explanation for this error? No. Because the Judge made an addition error, he declared Jayalalithaa's disproportionate assets to be below ten per cent and acquitted her. Had he added the figures correctly, the assets accumulated beyond her income would have been 76 per cent. Would Jayalalithaa then be liable for punishment or not?

More than that, in an interview published today in the English daily *The Economic Times*, Karnataka Special Public Prosecutor B. V. Acharya said that, legally, arithmetically and on a proper calculation of the facts, Jayalalithaa had accumulated assets 200 per cent beyond her income. What is their answer to that? By remaining silent for so many days on these central questions arising in everyone's mind, do they not confirm their own guilt?

Justice Dhanapalan of the Madras High Court, who retired two days ago, said at his farewell: “The judiciary is permanent. The High Court is permanent. Judges and lawyers will come and go. We must work carefully so that no disrepute attaches to this institution. If the public loses faith in the judiciary, there can be no worse condition. We must act in a manner that inspires public confidence in the judiciary. No danger will arise from those outside it. The danger will come from people like us within the judiciary. We must therefore act with vigilance.” We should consider how closely Justice Dhanapalan's observations fit the situation that has now arisen in the country.
On page 853 of his judgment, Justice Kumaraswamy wrote:

> The Accused No. 1 has claimed that she has received a sum of Rs. Two Crores and Fifteen Lakhs. Accused No. 1 has performed marriage of her foster son - Accused No. 3 and during her birth-day, she received gifts from the Party workers, friends, well-wishers etc. She has also claimed foreign remittance of Rs. 77 lakhs. Taking into consideration all these aspects, in my view, the income that arises from the gifts in my estimation will be around Rs. One Crore and Fifty Lakhs.

In other words, Justice Kumaraswamy said: “The first accused, Jayalalithaa, claims to have received Rs. 2.15 crore as gifts. At the marriage of her foster son Sudhakaran and on her own birthday, she received gifts from party workers, friends and well-wishers. She also claims to have received Rs. 77 lakh from abroad. Taking all these circumstances into consideration, I estimate Jayalalithaa's income from gifts at approximately Rs. 1.5 crore.”

It is unlawful for a public servant to accept gifts. As Chief Minister, Jayalalithaa is a public servant. She admits and accounts for Rs. 2.15 crore received as gifts, including Rs. 77 lakh from abroad. When Justice Kumaraswamy himself states in the judgment that he has counted this and recognised Rs. 1.5 crore as income from gifts, is it just for Jayalalithaa, a public servant, to receive property worth crores of rupees as gifts? Is it lawful?
Was it proper for the Judge to accept those gifts as legitimate income and use them to set aside Jayalalithaa's conviction?

At the same time, compare this with the continuing case against a private television company and its directors, even though the company received money through bank cheques on the basis of proper loan documents, repaid the loan with interest by cheque, and duly disclosed it in its income-tax returns!

I mentioned above Justice Kumaraswamy's addition error, did I not? Now listen to another curious feature of that calculation. The second item among the bank loans attributed to Jayalalithaa's side is an agricultural loan of Rs. 3.75 crore, taken in the name of Gunaboosani for the Kodanad Estate. How was that counted as Jayalalithaa's income? How the Kodanad Estate itself was acquired is a separate question. It was purchased then for only around Rs. 10 crore. Do you know its present value? The Kodanad tea estate extends over approximately 900 acres. Even if its present minimum market value is taken as Rs. 5 crore per acre, it is worth Rs. 4,500 crore.

Concealing all these true facts, somehow obtaining a judgment and pretending to the world that they are saints, then hurriedly assuming office as Chief Minister on the strength of that judgment: there is evidence and there are figures in abundance for everyone to understand who this person is and what kind of person she is. What is one to tell? What is one to leave out?
